directions.name: Steady-state Barrier / Handoff Retime
directions.hypothesis: Round 12 repaired the search-surface mismatch and restored the PTX microkernel as the dominant hot-band launch. The new best run at 24.16427231 ms slightly improved the exact scheduler-facing counters that still matter on this branch: active warps rose to 16.63%, barrier eased to 5.43%, and long-scoreboard eased to 7.17%, while DRAM stayed low at 12.44%. A source diff against `df5bac2` is now comment-only, so the restore and export-cleanup families are absorbed again and should be filtered out. The highest-value unabsorbed follow-on on the active PTX surface is therefore the narrow handoff seam around `cp_async_wait_group_0`, `__syncthreads()`, and the future-tile refill order. The prior barrier/handoff attempt should not be treated as negative evidence here because it was measured on the non-PTX sibling dispatch, not on the restored PTX microkernel that now owns the runtime.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual wait-group and barrier cadence in the active PTX hot-band steady-state loop, especially the handoff between MMA issue completion and future-tile refill.
directions.risk: Moderate. The family is bounded and now truly acts on the live PTX surface, but it still trades scheduler timing at the margin and can easily reshape barrier versus scoreboard without producing a measurable end-to-end win.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.164272 ms current best run
ranking_notes: Now that the PTX microkernel is back on the measured hot path, this family becomes a real active-branch probe again instead of a stale idea on the wrong surface.
Restore and export-cleanup families are filtered out because the current source is already semantically aligned with the best-known PTX surface.
It ranks first because it is the highest-confidence unabsorbed scheduler lever on the branch that just produced the new best custom run.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if another bounded handoff retime again only trades barrier against scoreboard without improving runtime on the restored PTX surface.

directions.name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
directions.hypothesis: With the PTX restore complete, the broader active-branch control family is coherent again as a second-ranked follow-on. The current source already absorbed the narrow export cleanup and exact restore, but the PTX hot-band path still lives at only 16.63% active warps with barrier and scoreboard both non-trivial. That leaves room for one more bounded control-path exploit that stays on the winning PTX surface while explicitly avoiding the already-tested B-first prologue and issue-grouping variants that flattened earlier. This family stays below dir_01 because it is broader and easier to accidentally overlap with already-measured sub-variants.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual PTX hot-band control-path overhead and consume-order friction inside the active one-K 128x128 microkernel, beyond the already-absorbed export cleanup and exact restore.
directions.risk: Moderate. The family still targets the hottest path, but prior sub-variants already flattened, so another control tweak must be materially different from the B-first prologue and issue-grouping experiments or it will likely collapse back into noise.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.164272 ms current best run
Kernel Name in ncu_details.csv for the dominant hot-band launch
ranking_notes: The PTX control family is actionable again because round 12 restored the branch it is supposed to tune.
It stays behind dir_01 because the narrower handoff seam is better matched to the current counter signature and carries less overlap with already-tested sub-variants.
Keeping it second preserves a real active-PTX fallback if the specific handoff retime stays flat.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the next control-path tweak replays an already-tested prologue or issue-order variant, or if it leaves the restored PTX surface runtime-flat again.

directions.name: Restore accepted grouped_rows=8 hot-band consumer ordering
directions.hypothesis: The queue should still carry one alternate PTX surface from `round_history` rather than collapsing entirely onto grouped_rows=4 scheduler micro-tunes. The grouped_rows=8 consumer-ordering restore remains the strongest such fallback after the surface repair: it is still open in the frontier, it is a genuine current-source delta, and it gives the loop a way to reopen a meaningfully different PTX locality regime if both active-branch exploits flatten. It stays third because round 12 just proved the grouped_rows=4 PTX surface can still produce the overall best runtime when it is actually the dominant measured kernel.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Consumer-side ordering and grouped-row locality inside the PTX hot-band microkernel, especially grouped CTA mapping and the export handoff under the grouped_rows=8 regime.
directions.risk: Moderate. This is a branch reset onto a historically slower PTX surface, so it is useful only as an auditable alternate state if the active grouped_rows=4 branch saturates again.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.164272 ms current best run
correctness pass rate across all 3 cases
ranking_notes: This stays in the set because the user explicitly asked to keep promising round-history states alive in the live queue.
It now ranks below the active PTX families because round 12 proved the grouped_rows=4 PTX surface still owns the best measured runtime when restored coherently.
Keeping it third preserves one real alternate PTX state without recommending an unnecessary branch reset immediately after a new best run.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the grouped_rows=8 restore remains materially behind the repaired grouped_rows=4 PTX surface without producing a clearly better scheduler signature.

notes: Round 13/100 audit: round 12 repaired the search-surface mismatch and restored `bf16_gemm_v1_tensor_core_fixed_hot_band_128x128_ptx_microkernel<(int)452>` as the dominant measured hot-band launch, producing a new best custom runtime at 24.16427231 ms. The current source is now semantically aligned with the previous best-known PTX surface, so restore and export-cleanup families are absorbed again and should be filtered out for this round. The ranking therefore returns to genuinely unabsorbed active-PTX follow-ons, while keeping one round-history PTX alternate alive in the live queue.
